title: Oak serving board with handle
subtitle: Paddle-shaped serving board in seasoned oak with a through handle
excerpt: A paddle-shaped serving board in seasoned oak: a wide cutting surface and a shaped handle bored through for hanging. Plane flat, shape the handle, apply board butter. A clean first seasoned-wood project.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Household Guide on kitchen board construction. Beeton's Book of Household Management on household boards and cutting surfaces.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber at stable moisture content. Essential for kitchen boards: green wood warps and checks as it dries.
The direction of the wood fibres. A serving board is planed along the grain for a clean surface.
A flat 45-degree bevel cut along an edge or corner to remove the sharp arris.

content.text: A serving board is used at the table for bread, cheese, and charcuterie. This paddle shape is 35 cm long: 25 cm of wide cutting surface narrowing to a 10 cm shaped handle. The board is made from
seasoned
oak; the
grain

should run along the board length. Oak is the best serving-board species for food safety: it has natural antimicrobial properties from the tannins, planes to a smooth surface, and takes a board-butter finish well.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

One board of seasoned oak: 35 cm long, 20 cm wide, 20 mm thick. The board face must be flat; a slight bow can be planed flat. Avoid boards with knots in the cutting surface. The handle section can include a small knot if it is sound and tight.
Tools
A smoothing plane (No. 4) for truing the face; a block plane for the
chamfers
and the handle shaping; a marking gauge and try-square for layout; a brace and bit (12 mm) for the hanging hole; a sloyd knife for the handle profile (optional); board butter.
Method

Flatten the board face with the No. 4 plane. Work at 45 degrees to the grain for the first passes to knock down any high spots, then finish along the grain. Check with a straight-edge: the face must be flat to within 0.5 mm.

Mark the board outline with a pencil: 25 cm wide cutting surface (the full board width), transitioning to a 5 cm wide handle over 5 cm, then the handle running the remaining 10 cm at 5 cm width. Draw both faces identically.
Cut the handle profile on the band saw, coping saw, or by scoring with the marking knife and chiseling to the line. A straight-sided handle is simplest; a slightly narrowed waist is more comfortable.
Chamfer the edges of the cutting surface with the block plane:
chamfer
at 45 degrees, 3 mm wide. Work with the
grain
direction. Chamfering removes the sharp arris and reduces the chance of the edge chipping in use.
Bore the hanging hole: 12 mm bore, centred 1.5 cm from the handle end. Bore from one face to halfway, then complete from the other face to prevent tearout.
Finishing

Sand the cutting surface through 120, 180, and 240 grit along the grain. Apply board butter: a beeswax-and-mineral-oil paste. Apply with a soft cloth, let stand 20 minutes, buff off. Re-apply monthly when in regular use.
Care
Rinse under warm water, towel-dry immediately. Never soak or dishwasher. Re-apply board butter when the surface feels rough or looks dry.
Variations
Ash instead of oak: pale, slightly softer, food-safe. Does not have oak's antimicrobial tannins.
Round board: cut the board face as a circle and drill the hanging hole in the centre.
Carved grip instead of shaped handle: whittle the handle section to an oval cross-section with the sloyd knife.

items.symptom: Board surface is rough after planing
items.cause: Planing against the grain; the plane is tearing the fibres
items.fix: Check the grain direction at the board edge. The grain lines should run downhill in the direction of plane travel. If tearing occurs, plane from the other end.

items.symptom: Chamfer is uneven in width along the edge
items.cause: Block plane not held at a consistent angle
items.fix: Use the block plane fence or tape the edge as a guide. Rest the plane body at 45 degrees against the edge and keep it constant throughout the stroke.

items.symptom: Board warps after the first few uses
items.cause: Board was not fully seasoned when purchased
items.fix: Apply board butter to all faces (top, bottom, and edges) equally. One-sided finishing causes unequal moisture exchange and warping.
